steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures around the blank
steps.content: On the left: an independent clause—”geophysicists like Dr. Estella Atekwana at the University of Delaware rely in part on data about the fault's physical dimensions and geological [blank]”. On the right: an independent clause—”the US Geological Survey compiles such data in several ways”—preceded by a prepositional phrase. These two clauses need to be connected or separated properly.

steps.title: Apply the sentence boundary rule
steps.content:
A period separates two independent clauses into two sentences. Choice C places a period after “features,” allowing the first sentence to close and the second to start with “For,” which introduces a specific example with the Imperial fault. The other choices fail: A and D create run-ons by omitting a boundary; B creates a comma splice (comma is insufficient without a coordinating conjunction).

That matches choice C — C is correct.
